why_it_feels_this_way: Soft PROFLY midsole delivers plush cushioning despite a relatively low stack. Strong energy return for a non-plated daily trainer. Very flexible and extremely light. Zero outsole rubber — the foam is directly exposed to the ground. Wide heel but low structural rigidity creates mixed stability signals.
avoid_if: Avoid if durability matters — zero outsole rubber means it wears through fast. Terrible in cold with massive firmness increase. Not for wet conditions — exposed foam has no grip when wet.
similar_to: HOKA Mach 6, ASICS Novablast 3, Saucony Endorphin Speed 3
common_issues: durability:zero outsole rubber — foam is directly exposed
cold:worst cold-weather firmness increase in the database
wet:exposed foam outsole is dangerous on wet surfaces

why_it_feels_this_way: A relatively light build with a mild rocker keeps turnover quick and smooth, while the midsole stays more lively than plush for faster daily running.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want maximal cushioning, as the ride is relatively firm and quick.
similar_to: adidas Adizero SL 2, New Balance FuelCell Rebel v5, PUMA Velocity NITRO 4
notable_detail: Uses a dual-density PROFLY midsole to balance speed and daily usability.
common_issues: durability:outsole rubber wears quickly
ride:less stable when fatigued

why_it_feels_this_way: Dual-foam midsole with soft PEBA primary layer and firmer EVA secondary creates a well-cushioned ride with decent forefoot energy return. Pebax plate adds propulsion. Extremely tall stack — among the tallest super trainers. Very high drop favours heel strikers. Pronounced rocker curve promotes aggressive forward roll. Torsional rigidity is maximum but heel counter is essentially zero — stable platform but no rear-foot lockdown. Light for its massive stack.
avoid_if: Avoid if you have Achilles sensitivity — the heel collar is a known irritant. Very narrow tapered toebox. Drop is much higher than brand claims. Not stable enough for some runners despite the tall stack.
similar_to: HOKA Mach X 3, Brooks Glycerin 22, Nike Invincible 3
notable_detail: HOKA's plated super trainer before the Mach X 3 update. Pebax plate rather than carbon — unusual choice. Excellent cold-weather performer — foam barely firms. Very tall stack flirts with maximalist territory. Excellent toebox and upper durability. Heel counter rated only 1/5 despite 5/5 torsional rigidity.
common_issues: heel:heel collar causes Achilles discomfort — common complaint
fit:small tapered toebox — narrow throughout
drop:measured drop significantly higher than brand claims

why_it_feels_this_way: A high-stack dual-foam midsole split by a Pebax plate and a pronounced rocker creates a smooth roll and steady pop for long tempo efforts without feeling brutally stiff.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want flexibility, as the plate and rocker create a stiff ride.
similar_to: adidas Adizero Boston 13, Saucony Endorphin Speed 5, Nike Zoom Fly 6
notable_detail: A plated trainer-racer hybrid built around PEBA foam for springy turnover.
common_issues: ride:plate feels intrusive at slow speeds
stability:less confidence on tight turns

why_it_feels_this_way: Dual-layer supercritical PEBA foam delivers very soft, high-energy cushioning. Energy return is near the top of all shoes tested. The defining feature is the ultra-aggressive rocker — the heel curvature is bold and forward-driving while the forefoot rocker begins early and climbs dramatically. Full-length forked carbon plate amplifies the propulsion. Very stiff through the plate. Generous forefoot stack provides cushioning for front-foot strikers.
avoid_if: Avoid if you dislike aggressive rockers — this is the most rockered race shoe in the database. Also not for wide feet as the fit is extremely narrow throughout. Runs half a size small.
similar_to: Nike Vaporfly Next% 3, ASICS Metaspeed Sky Paris
notable_detail: The most aggressively rockered super shoe available. The forked carbon plate adds heel flex while maintaining full-length propulsion. PEBA foam handles cold well with only modest firmness increase. Exceptional outsole durability for a race shoe. Semi-gusseted tongue is rare among super shoes.
common_issues: fit:extremely narrow throughout — among the narrowest race shoes tested
rocker:most aggressive rocker geometry in the database
sizing:runs half a size small — size up recommended

why_it_feels_this_way: Dual-layer PEBA foam (both layers soft) delivers world-class energy return — among the highest tested. Tall stack flirts with the World Athletics limit. Full-length stiff carbon plate amplifies propulsion. Higher drop than its predecessor benefits heel strikers. Aggressive rocker promotes strong forward roll but less extreme than the X2. Narrow platforms — especially the very narrow heel — prioritise speed over stability.
avoid_if: Avoid if weight is your top priority — heavier than most competing super shoes. Also not for wide feet — narrow throughout with a tight, tapered toebox. The aggressive rocker geometry won't suit all runners. Runs slightly small.
similar_to: ASICS Metaspeed Sky Tokyo, Nike Vaporfly 4
notable_detail: HOKA's most durable super shoe — unusual focus on longevity for a race shoe. PEBA foam barely firms in cold — virtually immune to temperature changes. Extremely breathable upper. Good outsole traction. Positioned more as a marathon racer than a 5K/10K weapon. Higher drop than the X2 makes it more heel-striker-friendly.
common_issues: weight:heavier than most competing super shoes
fit:tight tapered toebox — narrow throughout
rocker:aggressive forward roll may feel unusual for some runners

why_it_feels_this_way: Supercritical EVA foam is firmer than expected for a max-cushion shoe
avoid_if: Avoid if you want a bouncy or energetic ride — energy return is merely average. Also not for speed work given the weight and stiff build. Narrow toebox not for wide feet.
similar_to: HOKA Bondi 9, HOKA Clifton 10, Brooks Glycerin 22
notable_detail: HOKA's first supercritical EVA daily trainer — slots between Clifton and Bondi in cushioning. Features 360-degree reflective elements and excellent durability across the upper and outsole.
common_issues: energy:energy return is below expectations for a 2025 launch
weight:heavy for a daily trainer
fit:extremely narrow toebox

why_it_feels_this_way: Massive dual-foam stack with PEBA top layer and supercritical EVA base. Strong energy return for a super trainer. H-shaped carbon plate makes it extremely stiff. Pronounced continuous rocker is among the most aggressive in the database — essential given the colossal stack. Enormous platforms with maximum torsional and heel counter scores deliver unmatched stability.
avoid_if: Avoid if weight matters — it's the heaviest shoe in the database. Not for fast paces due to extreme stiffness. Very narrow toebox despite wide midsole platforms.
similar_to: ASICS Sonicblast, HOKA Mach X 3, ASICS Superblast 2
notable_detail: The most cushioned shoe in the HOKA lineup and one of the tallest in the database. The innovative H-shaped carbon plate provides propulsion while allowing some flexibility. Virtually immune to temperature changes in cold conditions.
common_issues: weight:excessively heavy — heaviest in the database
fit:very narrow toebox despite wide heel platform
stiffness:too stiff for anything but running

why_it_feels_this_way: Ultra-soft EVA foam — among the softest trail shoe midsoles ever tested — creates a deeply cushioned, protective ride. Energy return is below average despite the soft foam — this is a sinker, not a bouncer. Low drop and low stack keep you reasonably connected to the terrain. Moderate late-stage meta-rocker smooths transitions. Wide platforms but low torsional and heel counter rigidity create mixed stability. Vibram Megagrip outsole with decent lug depth.
avoid_if: Avoid in cold weather — the foam firms up dramatically, among the worst cold-weather performers ever tested. Also not ideal if you want any energy return. Not for wide feet despite the platform width.
similar_to: HOKA Speedgoat 6, Nike Zegama 2, Brooks Catamount 3
notable_detail: The Speedgoat that defined HOKA's trail reputation. Ultra-soft midsole is among the softest trail shoes ever tested. Vibram Megagrip outsole. Firms up catastrophically in cold — the worst cold-weather trail shoe tested. Available in 2 widths. Thin insole keeps you close to the foam.
common_issues: cold:firms up catastrophically in freezing conditions — worst cold-weather trail shoe tested
bounce:poor energy return despite ultra-soft foam
fit:not for wide feet despite platform width

why_it_feels_this_way: A protective midsole and grippy outsole combine for dependable traction and control, with a stable, planted platform that prioritises security and protection over agility and soft step-in plushness.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want agility or ground feel on technical trails.
similar_to: Altra Olympus 6, New Balance Fresh Foam X Hierro v9, The North Face Vectiv Enduris 3
notable_detail: A max-cushion trail icon known for Vibram grip and ultra-distance comfort.
common_issues: fit:snug forefoot for wider feet
ride:firm platform on long descents

why_it_feels_this_way: Full PEBA midsole (very soft) with dual parallel carbon plates delivers world-class energy return. Tall stack provides maximum trail protection. Stiff with maximum torsional rigidity. Moderate rocker avoids the aggressive curvature of road racers — better suited for uneven terrain. Vibram Megagrip outsole with deep lugs.
avoid_if: Avoid if you have wide feet — it's one of the narrowest trail shoes tested. The price is eye-watering. Also skip for easy runs or hiking — it's pure race equipment.
similar_to: Saucony Xodus Ultra 4, HOKA Speedgoat 6, Nike Ultrafly
notable_detail: The first Tecton with a full PEBA midsole — previous versions used EVA. Features an integrated ankle gaiter and MATRYX upper for debris protection. Dual parallel carbon plates inherited from the Tecton X2 with added side-stabilising midsole wings.
common_issues: fit:extremely narrow — not for average or wide feet
price:among the most expensive trail shoes ever
weight:heavier than predecessor despite premium materials

why_it_feels_this_way: Firmer ProFly EVA foam delivers a controlled
avoid_if: Avoid for ultra distances — the low stack height and moderate cushioning aren't designed for efforts beyond 30K. Also not suitable for technical or muddy trails where the lugs lack bite.
similar_to: HOKA Challenger 8, Nike Pegasus Trail 5, Saucony Peregrine 15
notable_detail: Exceptional outsole durability with among the lowest wear ever tested. The ProFly foam barely firms in cold
common_issues: protection:no rock plate means sharp stones are felt
grip:lugs underperform on wet and muddy terrain
fit:narrow toebox limits wide feet

why_it_feels_this_way: POWERFLOW PRO EVA foam is softer than average for trail shoes, delivering a well-balanced ride with good ground feel. Moderate stack avoids both the maximalist and minimalist extremes. Low drop encourages midfoot and forefoot striking. Very flexible throughout. No rocker geometry — flat, natural profile lets the foot work freely. Anatomical foot-shaped toebox is among the widest ever tested for a trail shoe.
avoid_if: Avoid if you're a heel striker — the low drop and lack of heel bevel work against rearfoot landing. Also not for ultra distances — the moderate stack doesn't provide enough cushioning for very long efforts. Slightly heavy for its stack height.
similar_to: HOKA Torrent 3, Saucony Peregrine 14
notable_detail: UK brand with an anatomical, foot-shaped toebox — among the widest tested for any trail shoe. Innovative TPU pellet insole (BOOMERANG) is much thicker than standard, adding significant underfoot comfort. EVA foam handles cold surprisingly well for its type. Rock plate included for underfoot protection.
common_issues: weight:slightly heavy for its stack height
stability:very low torsional rigidity and narrow heel — minimal structure
fit:runs slightly small — size up recommended

why_it_feels_this_way: Dual-foam Super Boom midsole with full-length T1000 carbon plate. Soft, bouncy foam creates a highly cushioned and responsive ride with world-class energy return — draws comparisons to the Alphafly and Endorphin Elite. Tall stack with low drop favours midfoot strikers. Scooping carbon plate geometry. Upper fit is sloppy and difficult to lock down — the main weakness. Smooth, floaty ride that works across all paces. Extremely accessible for a race shoe — comfortable and forgiving rather than demanding.
avoid_if: Avoid if you need a locked-down, precise fit — the upper is sloppy and hard to secure. Not widely available in Western markets. Heavy for a super shoe at its price point. May feel too soft for runners wanting a firm, aggressive racer.
similar_to: Nike Alphafly 3, Saucony Endorphin Elite 2, Nike Vaporfly Next% 4
notable_detail: Li-Ning's answer to the Vaporfly — a comfortable, bouncy carbon super shoe that's more accessible than aggressive Western racers. T1000 carbon plate. Available primarily through Chinese retailers. Remarkable value compared to Western super shoes.
common_issues: fit:sloppy upper difficult to lock down
availability:not widely available in Western markets
weight:heavy for a super shoe

why_it_feels_this_way: Triple-layer midsole with soft race foam top layer, Super Boom Capsule middle layer, and firmer beaded PEBA base. The Super Boom Capsule is the star — delivers explosive, trampoline-like bounce that reviewers describe as transcendent. Very tall stack. Fiberglass wing plate adds midfoot stability. Described as the most fun running shoe of all time by multiple reviewers. Best for easy through tempo pace. Slightly unstable due to the extreme softness and bounce.
avoid_if: Avoid if you need stability — the extreme bounce creates some instability. Not widely available in Western markets. Sizing can be inconsistent. Not a speed shoe despite the bounce — maxes out around tempo pace.
similar_to: ASICS Megablast, Nike Vomero Premium, R.A.D UFO
notable_detail: Chinese brand making waves in the hobby jogger community. Super Boom Capsule technology claims 95% energy return. Triple-layer midsole is unique in the market. Available primarily through Chinese retailers and Amazon. Outstanding value at ~£130.
common_issues: availability:not widely available in Western markets
stability:extreme bounce creates some instability
sizing:inconsistent sizing across production runs

why_it_feels_this_way: Enerzy XP TPEE foam delivers a bouncy, responsive ride with a carbon-infused nylon plate adding structure and snap. The nylon plate is more flexible than full carbon — less aggressive, more forgiving. Tall stack with low drop. Extreme lateral flare creates unusual stability for runners prone to ankle rolling — but can feel clunky for others. Smooth Speed Assist geometry provides guided transitions. Very light for its stack. Thin monofilament upper is almost see-through. Sits between racer and trainer — not clearly one or the other.
avoid_if: Avoid if you prefer medial stability — the extreme lateral flare actively drives pronation. Runs short — size up half a size. Thin upper can be scratchy without socks. Loud midsole noise on footstrike. Expensive for a super trainer at £250.
similar_to: Mizuno Wave Rebellion Flash 3, ASICS Megablast, PUMA Deviate NITRO 4
notable_detail: Mizuno's successor to the Rebellion series — the most approachable of the new Hyperwarp racing trio. Carbon-infused nylon plate (not full carbon). Enerzy XP TPEE foam. Extreme lateral flare is unique in the market. Smooth Speed Assist geometry. Runs short — size up. G3 rubber outsole.
common_issues: fit:runs short — size up half a size
flare:extreme lateral flare is polarising — great for ankle instability, clunky for others
noise:loud midsole on footstrike

why_it_feels_this_way: The layered midsole is highly protective but not truly plush, so it compresses with a deeper, damped feel under load, with the wave structure adding some shape without making it feel rigid.
avoid_if: Avoid if you value cornering stability, as the tall stack and plate can feel awkward.
similar_to: On Cloudmonster 2, PUMA MagMax NITRO, ASICS Superblast 2
notable_detail: A plated super-trainer blending Enerzy foam with a carbon-infused Wave plate.
common_issues: stability:rocker feels awkward at slower paces
ride:less controlled when fatigued

why_it_feels_this_way: Enerzy NXT foam has been downgraded from supercritical TPU to EVA — a dramatic reduction in energy return. Energy return is among the worst in the database despite the premium foam branding. However, the foam is soft with a tall stack and very generous forefoot height, creating a deeply cushioned ride. Low drop suits midfoot strikers. Moderate rocker with smooth transitions. Flexible and light. Sock-like knit upper is extremely narrow and snug — designed for narrow feet only.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want any bounce or energy — the downgrade from supercritical TPU to EVA is dramatic. Only for narrow feet — the sock-like upper is extremely restrictive. Poor upper durability across toebox and heel. Not as fun or energetic as the original Neo Zen.
similar_to: Mizuno Neo Zen, adidas Adizero EVO SL, Saucony Ride 19
notable_detail: A disappointing foam downgrade from the original — Enerzy NXT swapped from supercritical TPU to EVA, destroying the energy return. Sock-like knit upper provides excellent lockdown for narrow feet. Handles cold well. Very generous forefoot stack for midfoot/forefoot strikers. Reflective elements.
common_issues: bounce:energy return collapsed from v1 due to foam downgrade from TPU to EVA
fit:extremely narrow — only for narrow feet
durability:poor across toebox and heel padding

why_it_feels_this_way: A big, highly compliant midsole gives a very soft, pillowy landing, but the foam rebounds with a clean pop rather than a dull sink. With a tall stack and less inherent guidance, it can feel a bit wobbly when you are not running straight, so the ride is plush and lively but not especially planted.
avoid_if: Avoid if you prefer firm, structured rides, as the foam is very soft and flexible.
similar_to: Nike Pegasus Premium, New Balance Fresh Foam X 1080 v14, HOKA Bondi 9
notable_detail: Showcases Mizuno’s modern Enerzy foam in a highly cushioned, minimalist build.
common_issues: fit:low-volume upper for high insteps
ride:stiff forefoot limits natural flex

why_it_feels_this_way: Dual-foam midsole with firm Enerzy and softer Enerzy NXT creates a controlled
avoid_if: Avoid if you're a midfoot or forefoot striker — the very high drop works against a forward-leaning stride. Also not ideal if weight matters
similar_to: Brooks Adrenaline GTS 24, ASICS GT-2000 14, Saucony Guide 18
notable_detail: Upgraded Enerzy NXT foam boosts energy return from previous version but still sits below average. Full-coverage X10 outsole with thick rubber makes this one of the most durable shoes in the database.
common_issues: weight:still feels heavy despite improvements
drop:very high drop not suitable for non-heel strikers
ride:foam still lacks energy and bounce despite upgrade

why_it_feels_this_way: A dependable stability trainer that prioritises controlled comfort over excitement. The Enerzy NXT midsole feels soft enough for daily miles but returns very little energy, creating a smooth but flat ride. The Wave Plate in the heel provides structural stability without making the shoe feel rigid. Updated lower drop and added forefoot foam make it more versatile than previous Inspires. Excellent outsole durability for high-mileage runners.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want any sense of bounce or responsiveness — the ride is comfortable but lifeless at faster paces. Not for runners who prefer lightweight or minimal shoes. Only addresses mild pronation, so severe overpronators should look elsewhere.
similar_to: Mizuno Wave Inspire 21, Brooks Adrenaline GTS 24, ASICS Gel Kayano 31
notable_detail: The lower drop compared to previous Inspires opens the shoe up to midfoot strikers for the first time in the series. Outstanding outsole durability. The Wave Plate is a stability device, not a propulsion plate.
common_issues: bounce:flat ride with minimal energy return
weight:heavier than most modern stability shoes
pronation:only addresses mild pronation

why_it_feels_this_way: Ultra-soft top layer foam is one of the softest ever tested
avoid_if: Avoid if you're a heel striker — the extreme heel bevel and narrow heel platform make rearfoot landing risky. Also not for wide feet.
similar_to: Mizuno Wave Rebellion Pro 3, Saucony Endorphin Speed 5, HOKA Mach X 3
notable_detail: Record-breaking heel shock absorption — highest in the entire database. The unconventional geometry with extreme heel bevel and toe spring makes this one of the most distinctive rides available. Barely firms in cold conditions.
common_issues: geometry:extreme heel bevel not suitable for heel strikers
fit:narrow with low toebox height
durability:heel padding scored poorly

why_it_feels_this_way: Extreme stack height and a very aggressive geometry create a soft, springy feel that rewards fast, forward running, but the unstable platform and minimal ground feel make it demanding if you are not landing efficiently.
avoid_if: Avoid if you heel strike heavily, need stability, want an easy all-round racer, or prefer a natural, flexible ride - it works best when you stay forward and commit to the geometry.
similar_to: adidas Adizero Adios Pro 4, Saucony Endorphin Elite 2, Nike Vaporfly Next% 4
notable_detail: Mizuno’s aggressive geometry is the whole point: the shoe is designed to keep you moving forward, with a rocker and midsole shape that works best when you stay on the front half of the shoe. If you land back on the heel, it feels clumsy and inefficient - the ride only clicks when you commit to a forward landing and quick turnover.
common_issues: stability:aggressive geometry feels unstable
ride:awkward if cadence drops

why_it_feels_this_way: New Infinion TPEE foam is dramatically softer than the v14 — one of the softest midsoles ever tested. Ultra-plush marshmallow feel with tall stack. A major leap in energy return from the old Fresh Foam X. Low drop suits midfoot strikers. Flexible with no plate. Narrower forefoot platform limits stability.
avoid_if: Avoid if you need stability — the ultra-soft foam and narrow forefoot platform can feel wobbly. Also polarising — runners who prefer a controlled or firm ride will dislike the marshmallow sensation.
similar_to: New Balance Fresh Foam X 1080 v14, HOKA Bondi 9, Nike Invincible 3
notable_detail: First 1080 with non-EVA foam — the Infinion TPEE material marks a generational shift for the series. Virtually immune to temperature changes — best cold-weather performance in the database. Available in 4 widths including X-Wide.
common_issues: stability:ultra-soft foam feels wobbly for some runners
polarising:cloud-like feel is love-it-or-hate-it
price:expensive with inconsistent pricing across markets

why_it_feels_this_way: Fresh Foam X EVA/TPEE blend midsole delivers soft, compliant cushioning that sinks in on landing — described as marshmallow-like. But the foam lacks energy return — it bottoms out rather than springing back. Tall stack with a heavily rockered profile provides smooth, guided transitions. Positioned between the 880 and 1080 in NB's lineup at a lower price. Gusseted tongue. Wider fit with decent toebox room. Best for easy and recovery runs — struggles at faster paces where the foam can't respond quickly enough.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want bounce or responsiveness — the foam bottoms out and lacks energy return. Not for speed work. Slightly wider fit may not suit narrow feet. The rocker may feel too guided for runners wanting natural toe-off.
similar_to: New Balance 1080 v15, New Balance Fresh Foam X 880 v15, HOKA Clifton 10
notable_detail: New Balance's comfort-first daily trainer at an accessible price point. Fresh Foam X (not the newer Infinion) keeps costs down. Heavily rockered. Gusseted tongue. Celebrity endorsements from Gabby Thomas and Josh Allen. £130 UK price.
common_issues: bounce:foam bottoms out — lacks energy return
speed:struggles at faster paces
foam:Fresh Foam X not the newer Infinion — older technology

why_it_feels_this_way: A balanced, slightly cushioned feel that sits in the middle of the spectrum: not especially soft or bouncy, but smooth and steady with a more natural transition and moderate ground feedback.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want a lively tempo shoe, very soft recovery cushioning, or a snappier rocker-driven ride.
similar_to: Nike Pegasus 41, Brooks Ghost 17, ASICS Gel Contend 9
notable_detail: New Balance positions it as a value daily trainer built around Fresh Foam cushioning and a simple, durable outsole, aimed at comfort per pound rather than standout speed feel.
common_issues: ride:firm for heavier runners
use_case:lacks versatility beyond easy mileage

full_name: New Balance Fresh Foam X 1080 v13
why_it_feels_this_way: Fresh Foam X delivers the softest midsole ever tested — an absolute marshmallow. Moderate stack doesn't reach maximalist territory but feels extremely cushioned due to the foam's extraordinary softness. Energy return is average — the EVA base limits bounce despite incredible softness. Wide platforms but low torsional rigidity means limited stability despite the width. Flexible throughout.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want bounce or speed — EVA foam delivers softness without energy return. Also not stable despite the wide platform due to the extreme foam softness.
similar_to: New Balance Fresh Foam X 1080 v14, HOKA Bondi 8, Nike Invincible 3
notable_detail: The softest midsole ever measured by lab testing. A radical redesign that shifted the 1080 from an average daily trainer to a max-cushion shoe. Outsole durability is exceptional with among the lowest wear ever recorded. Available in 4 widths.
common_issues: stability:extremely soft foam provides no structural support
speed:EVA foam lacks energy return for faster paces
cold:firms up more than average in cold weather

full_name: New Balance Fresh Foam X 1080 v14
why_it_feels_this_way: Its high Fresh Foam X stack and refined rocker dampen impact smoothly, leading to a cushioned ride that feels slightly muted rather than energetic.
avoid_if: Avoid if you dislike very soft, muted cushioning or prefer a roomy toe box.
similar_to: Nike Vomero Plus, ASICS Gel Nimbus 27, Saucony Triumph 23
notable_detail: Updates the 1080 with more stack and softness, shifting it closer to a true max-cushion daily trainer.
common_issues: ride:too soft for heavier runners
stability:can feel unstable late in runs

full_name: New Balance Fresh Foam X 860 v14
why_it_feels_this_way: Dual-layer Fresh Foam X midsole with a very soft primary layer delivers a plush ride
avoid_if: Avoid if upper durability matters to you — the heel padding is among the worst tested. Also skip if you prefer a simpler midsole design.
similar_to: Brooks Adrenaline GTS 24, ASICS GT-2000 14, Saucony Guide 18
notable_detail: Uses an innovative dual-layer Fresh Foam X setup where the primary layer is ultra-soft and the secondary heel layer is nearly twice as firm
common_issues: durability:heel padding is among the worst tested
breathability:limited airflow
weight:heavier than expected for its category

full_name: New Balance Fresh Foam X 880 v15
why_it_feels_this_way: Fresh Foam X EVA foam is moderately soft with a dramatically taller stack than any previous 880 — pushed firmly into maximalist territory. Energy return is below average — the familiar EVA base limits bounce. Maximum torsional rigidity marks a drastic shift from previous 880 models. Very low drop is unusual for this series. Rockered geometry is new to the 880. Heavy. Feels like a budget-friendly 1080 rather than the balanced daily trainer the 880 used to be.
avoid_if: Avoid if you loved the old balanced 880 — this is a completely different shoe. Heavy. Below-average energy return. Thin outsole rubber coverage raises durability concerns despite decent wear test results. Not for speed work.
similar_to: New Balance 1080 v15, New Balance Fresh Foam X 1080 v14, Brooks Ghost 17
notable_detail: A bold transformation that moves the 880 from balanced daily trainer into maximalist territory. Maximum torsional rigidity matches carbon-plated shoes — a drastic shift. Very low drop is new for this series. Rockered geometry added. Handles cold well. Available in multiple widths.
common_issues: weight:heavy for a daily trainer
identity:no longer the balanced 880 — now a budget 1080
durability:thin outsole rubber coverage raises concerns

full_name: New Balance Fresh Foam X Hierro v9
why_it_feels_this_way: A tall, protective midsole and a broad platform keep things calm on uneven ground, with a steady rebound rather than a squishy feel and a noticeably substantial, protective ride.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want a light or agile trail shoe.
similar_to: The North Face Vectiv Enduris 3, HOKA Challenger 8, ASICS Metafuji Trail
notable_detail: A max-cushion trail model built for comfort and protection over long distances.
common_issues: weight:feels heavy on longer trail runs
ride:soft midsole reduces precision on rocky ground

full_name: New Balance Fresh Foam X More v6
why_it_feels_this_way: An oversized Fresh Foam X midsole on a broad platform spreads impact widely for ultra-soft cushioning with built-in stability, but the huge stack feels more cruiser than nimble.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want ground feel or a natural ride, as the towering, soft platform isolates you from the road.
similar_to: HOKA Bondi 9, Brooks Glycerin Max, Nike Vomero 18
notable_detail: New Balance’s most cushioned trainer, built around an ultra-thick Fresh Foam X midsole tuned for comfort.
common_issues: stability:wobbly on cambered roads
weight:feels cumbersome over distance

why_it_feels_this_way: Ultra-soft FuelCell foam — one of the softest in the database. Strong energy return for a non-plated shoe through both heel and forefoot. Very low stack keeps you connected to the ground despite the soft foam. The most flexible shoe in its generation. Ultra-light. Wide roomy fit accommodates broader feet.
avoid_if: Avoid if you need stability — low torsional rigidity and weak heel counter despite wide platforms. Toebox durability is very poor. Firms up badly in cold — a known FuelCell weakness.
similar_to: NB FuelCell Rebel v5, HOKA Mach 5, ASICS Novablast 5
common_issues: stability:low torsional rigidity and weak heel counter despite wide platforms
durability:very poor toebox durability
cold:firms up badly — a known FuelCell weakness

why_it_feels_this_way: A very light FuelCell midsole compresses easily for a soft, fast-feeling ride with some rebound, and the lower guidance and slimmer build make it feel agile and more connected than max-cushion cruisers.
avoid_if: Avoid if you need structure or stability, as the soft foam and flexible build prioritise fun over control.
similar_to: adidas Adizero EVO SL, HOKA Mach 6, PUMA Velocity NITRO 4
notable_detail: Famed for its ultra-light, soft FuelCell foam that feels playful across a wide pace range.
common_issues: durability:midsole loses pop quickly
stability:less stable when fatigued

full_name: New Balance FuelCell SuperComp Elite v5
why_it_feels_this_way: A high-stack FuelCell midsole with a stiff carbon plate keeps you moving efficiently at pace, but the tall geometry trades some stability for speed and cushioning.
avoid_if: Avoid if you are not racing or doing hard workouts, as the setup is aggressive.
similar_to: Saucony Endorphin Pro 4, Nike Alphafly Next% 3, adidas Adizero Adios Pro 4
notable_detail: Uses Energy Arc technology to create a rolling, highly propulsive marathon shoe.

notable_detail: Exposes the inconsistency in PUMA's NITRO branding — same foam name delivers vastly different energy return across models. Among the tallest shoes ever made. Wide forefoot platform provides strong stability. PumaGrip outsole delivers excellent traction. Handles cold well. Lighter than its predecessor.
common_issues: bounce:energy return is very poor — among the worst for a premium shoe
fit:narrow tapered toebox limits toe splay
ride:very stiff underfoot feel

why_it_feels_this_way: A tall, high-stack NITRO setup prioritises protection with a more damped, steady feel rather than deep softness, while the broad base and rocker help smooth and stabilise the ride.
avoid_if: Avoid if you prefer low-stack shoes, as the midsole height is extreme.
similar_to: Brooks Glycerin Max, Nike Vomero Plus, Saucony Triumph 23
notable_detail: A bold max-cushion design with a massive slab of NITRO foam that still feels lively.

ride:softness reduces stability when tired

why_it_feels_this_way: A thick NITRO midsole prioritises comfort and impact reduction, with moderate energy return.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want a nimble or fast-feeling shoe, as it is heavy and comfort-focused.

notable_detail: A durable max-cushion trainer built around a thick NITRO foam platform.

ride:lacks responsiveness for faster efforts

why_it_feels_this_way: NITRO foam primary layer is moderately soft but the very firm carrier foam underneath significantly mutes the ride — described as less springy than other NITRO implementations. Energy return is below average. Average stack with moderate drop. Flat non-rockered geometry encourages natural foot movement. Good flexibility. Excellent outsole traction — one of the best grip scores in the budget category.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want bounce — the firm carrier foam mutes the primary NITRO foam's potential. Narrow fit throughout, especially the toebox. Drop may feel too steep for forefoot/midfoot strikers wanting lower-drop options.

common_issues: foam:firm carrier foam mutes the primary NITRO layer's bounce
fit:narrow throughout — especially the toebox
drop:steep drop may not suit forefoot/midfoot strikers

why_it_feels_this_way: NITRO foam gives a lively toe-off in a light build, but the relatively neutral platform and limited guidance keep stability feeling only moderate.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want very soft cushioning, as the ride is balanced and slightly firm.
similar_to: adidas Adizero SL 2, New Balance FuelCell Rebel v5, HOKA Mach 6
notable_detail: Known for excellent grip and a well-rounded NITRO foam ride at a competitive weight.

ride:firm forefoot at slower paces

why_it_feels_this_way: Dual-layer full-length PEBA blend midsole with a very soft top layer and firmer bottom layer. The heel is especially soft with massive stack creating a deeply cushioned, marshmallow-like sensation. Strong energy return — bouncy and responsive, drawing comparisons to the Megablast and Vomero Plus. No plate, but moderate stiffness with a smooth rockered geometry aids transitions. Very wide flared base creates a stable platform that prevents the tall stack from feeling wobbly. Best suited for easy runs through tempo pace. Struggles at faster efforts.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want a fast-feeling shoe — this maxes out at tempo pace and struggles on the track. The excessively wide flared sole feels clunky at faster paces. Very loud and squeaky on footstrike. Not for walking — unstable when not running. Divisive aesthetics.
similar_to: ASICS Superblast 3, ASICS Megablast, Nike Vomero Plus
notable_detail: R.A.D's debut running shoe from a UK brand originally known for CrossFit footwear. Dual-density PEBA blend midsole without a plate. CPU outsole is lighter than conventional rubber. Early reports suggest exceptional foam durability past 1000 miles. Gusseted tongue. Neon colourways only.
common_issues: speed:struggles at anything faster than tempo pace

aesthetics:divisive neon-only colourways

why_it_feels_this_way: The high-stack Energy Surge midsole cushions impact well and maintains a steady, neutral ride profile.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want ground feel, as the shoe prioritises cushioning and roll-through.
similar_to: ASICS Superblast 2, Skechers Aero Burst, Nike Vomero Plus
notable_detail: Salomon’s most cushioned road shoe, using a high-stack Energy Foam midsole.

stability:less confidence on road camber

why_it_feels_this_way: A high stack with a soft, energetic midsole gives a cushioned, bouncy feel for long runs, while the relatively low ground feel and tapered fit keep it feeling more like a modern max trainer than a stable cruiser.

similar_to: ASICS Superblast 2, adidas Adizero Prime X3 Strung, Nike Vomero Plus

common_issues: fit:snug midfoot for wider feet
ride:stiff transition on road

why_it_feels_this_way: Road-to-trail crossover based on the Aero Glide 4 platform with a gravel-specific outsole. Energy Surge foam midsole provides moderate cushioning and energy return. Tall stack for trail protection. Moderate rocker aids transitions on varied terrain. Designed for gravel paths, fire roads, and light trail — not technical terrain. Fills the gap between road super trainers and aggressive trail shoes.
avoid_if: Avoid for technical trails — the outsole is designed for gravel and fire roads, not rocky or muddy terrain. Limited data available — very new release. Not for road-only runners who won't use the trail outsole.
similar_to: Salomon Aero Glide 3, Salomon Ultra Glide 2, adidas Adizero EVO SL ATR
notable_detail: Salomon's road-to-trail crossover — the Aero Glide platform adapted for gravel and mixed terrain. Energy Surge foam. Gravel-specific outsole. Very limited reviews available at launch. Described as a favourite all-terrain shoe by Tom's Guide after 55 miles of testing.
common_issues: data:very limited reviews available — new release
terrain:designed for gravel and fire roads — not technical trail
identity:road shoe adapted for trail — may not excel at either

why_it_feels_this_way: EnergyCell EVA foam is firm and functional — designed for protection rather than comfort or bounce. No energy return to speak of — the foam is a platform, not a spring. Low stack keeps you very close to the ground with maximum trail feedback. Classic flat non-rockered profile. Aggressive deep lugs provide exceptional grip on soft, muddy, and loose terrain — the Speedcross's defining feature. Stiff. Narrow fit throughout. Quicklace system. Designed for short-to-mid distance technical trail racing, not ultra distances.
avoid_if: Avoid on hard surfaces — the aggressive lugs feel harsh and wear quickly on road or hardpack. Not for wide feet. No cushion for long distances. Essentially zero energy return. Heavy for a trail racing shoe.
similar_to: Salomon Speedcross 6, Salomon Speedcross 7, Inov-8 Trailfly
notable_detail: The iconic trail shoe that defined aggressive grip for a generation. Deep chevron lugs for mud, snow, and loose terrain. Quicklace system. Contagrip outsole. Narrow, race-oriented fit. Not a cushioned or bouncy shoe — pure grip and protection. Still widely available at discount.
common_issues: grip:aggressive lugs are harsh and wear quickly on hard surfaces
cushion:essentially no cushioning or energy return
fit:narrow throughout — not for wide feet

why_it_feels_this_way: A firm, very direct midsole and an aggressive lugged outsole create maximum bite and control on soft, muddy ground, but the ride feels rigid and intrusive on hard surfaces with almost no bounce.
avoid_if: Avoid if you run mainly on hard surfaces, as the aggressive lugs are intrusive.
similar_to: Saucony Peregrine 15, Nike Pegasus Trail 5, HOKA Challenger 8
notable_detail: Famous for deep lugs and narrow fit, excelling in mud and soft ground.
common_issues: use_case:overly aggressive for hardpack trails
ride:firm feel on longer distances

why_it_feels_this_way: EnergyFOAM (EVA+OBC) is soft for a trail shoe — well into the plush end of the trail spectrum. Energy return is above average for trail shoes but still moderate overall. Low-to-moderate stack keeps you connected without maximalist bulk. Reverse Camber geometry (subtle rocker) provides smooth heel-to-toe transitions for a trail shoe. Narrow platforms — especially the forefoot — favour agility and precision over stability. Flexible and light for its cushion level.
avoid_if: Avoid in cold weather — the EVA+OBC foam firms up dramatically, among the worst cold-weather performers tested. Also skip for hard rocky terrain — outsole durability is poor and thin rubber wears quickly. Not for wide feet — narrower than average throughout.
similar_to: Salomon Pulsar Trail, HOKA Speedgoat 5
notable_detail: Contagrip rubber outsole performs well on dry and moderate terrain. Salomon Quicklace system for fast lacing. Very breathable upper scored highly — a genuine summer trail option. Semi-gusseted tongue is exceptionally thick and comfortable. Addressed the v1's overly stiff heel counter.
common_issues: cold:foam firms up dramatically in freezing conditions — among the worst tested
durability:outsole wears faster than average with thin rubber
fit:narrower than average throughout — not for wide feet

why_it_feels_this_way: PWRRUN PB (Pebax) foam delivers strong energy return — competitive with the EVO SL. But the foam is surprisingly firm for a PEBA compound, creating an energetic ride that prioritises propulsion over plush comfort. Tall maximalist stack with aggressive SpeedRoll rocker that constantly pushes you forward. Maximum torsional rigidity with very wide forefoot platform provides strong stability for such an energetic shoe. Light for its stack. Barely firms in cold — among the best winter performers. Fantastic outsole durability.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want a soft, cloud-like feel — this is firm despite the tall stack and PEBA foam. The SpeedRoll rocker can feel intrusive and overly guided for some runners. Not ideal for fast interval work — the Speed 5 is better for that. Narrow toebox.
similar_to: adidas Adizero EVO SL, ASICS Novablast 5, Nike Pegasus Plus
notable_detail: Saucony's answer to the EVO SL — a Pebax super trainer at a competitive price. PWRRUN PB foam barely firms in cold — virtually immune to temperature changes. Exceptional outsole durability with minimal wear. SpeedRoll rocker geometry from the Endorphin series. Insane value for the energy return level.

rocker:SpeedRoll rocker can feel intrusive and overly guided
fun:described as not as fun as other super trainers

why_it_feels_this_way: An ultra-soft, high-rebound midsole paired with a very stiff carbon plate creates a huge spring off the forefoot, but the tall race setup can feel unstable and offers almost no ground feel.

similar_to: adidas Adizero Adios Pro 4, Nike Vaporfly Next% 4, PUMA Fast-R NITRO Elite 3

Full-length carbon plate. Very stiff through the plate. Light — among the lightest super shoes. Semi-gusseted tongue is rare among racers. Handles cold poorly — firms up significantly.

notable_detail: Saucony's carbon super shoe before the Pro 4 update. Same PWRRUN PB foam as the Speed 3. Semi-gusseted tongue is rare for a race shoe. Ultra-light. Firms up significantly in cold — unusual for Pebax. Non-removable insole.

why_it_feels_this_way: A stable, accessible carbon racer that prioritises control over raw speed. The full-length carbon plate with Saucony's signature Speedroll geometry creates a strong forward-rolling sensation without feeling twitchy or unstable. Light and fast with strong energy return, particularly suited to heel strikers thanks to the pronounced heel flare.

why_it_feels_this_way: PWRRUN PB foam with nylon plate delivers excellent energy return — near super shoe territory. Moderate stack. Balanced stability without any standout structural features. why_it_feels_this_way: Dual-foam midsole with PWRRUN PB (Pebax) primary and very firm PWRRUN frame. why_it_feels_this_way: Soft foam with moderate stack delivers cushioned landings with decent shock absorption. Energy return is very poor — among the worst tested for a shoe at this price. Moderate drop. Subtle rocker provides smooth transitions. Flexible and average weight. The standout feature is the extra-roomy toebox — among the widest ever tested for a road shoe. Poor breathability. Handles cold well.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want any bounce or energy — among the worst performers tested. Poor breathability. Heel padding durability is very poor. Non-gusseted tongue. Runs slightly small.
similar_to: Altra Torin 8, Topo Athletic Atmos, Altra Experience Flow 2
notable_detail: Topo's wide-toebox daily trainer with among the widest and tallest toebox measurements for any road shoe. Soft foam but zero bounce — comfort-first, not performance. Handles cold well. Good outsole durability. Runs slightly small.

why_it_feels_this_way: Charged Cushioning EVA foam is extremely firm — among the hardest midsoles tested, positioned on the opposite end of the plush spectrum. Combined with a very thick outsole, the ride feels almost brick-like. Low stack keeps you very close to the ground. No energy return to speak of — the foam is functional but completely dead. Classic high-drop flat geometry aimed at heel strikers. Surprisingly wide and roomy fit with generous toebox splay.
avoid_if: Avoid if you want any cushion softness or energy return — this is one of the firmest, most lifeless shoes in the database. Not for long distances due to the very low forefoot stack. Firms up significantly in cold weather.
similar_to: Nike Winflo 11, Saucony Axon 3
notable_detail: The cheapest shoe in the database — genuine budget entry point for casual runners, walkers, and gym use. Exceptionally thick dual-layer insole adds comfort the midsole can't provide. Very roomy fit accommodates wide feet. Outsole durability is solid for the price. Runs slightly small.
